title: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections
authors: David C. Hooper, Erica S. Shenoy, Alyssa R. Letourneau, Ramy H. Elshaboury

items.subtopic: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections
items.title: Importance of Appropriate Antimicrobial Use to Prevent Resistance
items.content: Antimicrobial agents are crucial for reducing mortality and enhancing quality of life, but their use can promote drug resistance. Therefore, careful selection based on the patient's clinical presentation, underlying conditions, and epidemiology is essential.
keyPoints: Antimicrobials reduce mortality and enhance quality of life.
Their misuse promotes drug resistance in pathogens and bystander organisms.
Optimal use requires understanding each drug's mechanism, spectrum, resistance mechanisms, pharmacology, and adverse effects.
Therapeutic choices should be refined with microbiologic information when available to minimize resistance risk.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections

items.subtopic: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections
items.title: Determining Therapy Duration Based on Infection Nature and Patient Response
items.content: The duration of antimicrobial therapy should be tailored according to the infection's nature and patient response, with shorter courses preferred to reduce resistance emergence.
keyPoints: Therapy durations depend on infection type and patient response.
Clinical studies inform duration choices when available.
Shorter treatment courses are less likely to promote resistance.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF ACTION (SEE TABLE 150-1) | INHIBITION OF CELL WALL SYNTHESIS
items.title: Why Bactericidal Agents are Preferred in Certain Infections
items.content: Bactericidal agents, which kill bacteria rather than merely inhibiting their growth, are generally preferred for treating infections in patients with impaired host defenses or at body sites with limited host defenses. This is because bacteriostatic agents rely on the patient's immune system to eradicate the pathogen, which may not be effective in such cases.
keyPoints: Bactericidal effect linked to cell lysis due to inhibition of cell-wall synthesis and action of autolysins.
Preferred for infections with impaired host defenses (e.g., neutropenia) or at sites like meningitis and endocarditis.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · MECHANISMS OF ACTION (SEE TABLE 150-1) | INHIBITION OF CELL WALL SYNTHESIS

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF ACTION (SEE TABLE 150-1) | INHIBITION OF CELL WALL SYNTHESIS
items.title: Mechanism of Action: $\beta$-Lactams and Glycopeptides
items.content: $\beta$-Lactam drugs target transpeptidase enzymes involved in peptidoglycan cross-linking, while glycopeptides bind to the terminal D-alanine residues of stem peptides, hindering polymerization. This dual mechanism disrupts cell wall synthesis and contributes to their bactericidal effect.
keyPoints: $\beta$-Lactams target transpeptidase enzymes (penicillin-binding proteins) involved in cross-linking.
Glycopeptides bind terminal D-alanine residues, hindering glycosyltransferase and transpeptidases.
Vancomycin also binds to lipid II intermediate, enhancing its potency.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · MECHANISMS OF ACTION (SEE TABLE 150-1) | INHIBITION OF CELL WALL SYNTHESIS

items.title: Aminoglycosides: Mechanism and Limitations
items.content: Aminoglycosides irreversibly bind to the 16S rRNA of the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking tRNA translocation and causing misreading at low concentrations. Their activity is reduced under anaerobic conditions due to decreased cellular uptake.
keyPoints: Bind irreversibly to 16S rRNA of the 30S ribosomal subunit
Block translocation of peptidyl tRNA from A to P site
Cause misreading of mRNA codons at low concentrations, leading to incorrect amino acids in peptide chain
Bactericidal action distinguishes them from most other protein synthesis inhibitors
Reduced activity under anaerobic conditions due to decreased uptake
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · INHIBITION OF PROTEIN SYNTHESIS

items.title: Tetracyclines: Mechanism and Resistance Circumvention
items.content: Tetracyclines reversibly bind to the 16S rRNA of the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl tRNA binding. Tigecycline and other derivatives can circumvent common resistance mechanisms.
keyPoints: Reversibly bind to 16S rRNA of the 30S ribosomal subunit
Block binding of aminoacyl tRNA to the A site, inhibiting peptide elongation
Active transport into bacterial cells enhances selectivity
Tigecycline and derivatives (eravacycline, omadacycline) resist common tetracycline resistance mechanisms
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · INHIBITION OF PROTEIN SYNTHESIS

items.title: Macrolides and Ketolides: Binding and Action
items.content: Macrolides and ketolides bind to the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking peptide chain translocation. This action is distinct from other protein synthesis inhibitors.
keyPoints: Bind to 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit
Block translocation of the growing peptide chain by binding to the exit tunnel
Include azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin (macrolides) and telithromycin (ketolide)
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · INHIBITION OF PROTEIN SYNTHESIS

items.subtopic: INHIBITION OF BACTERIAL METABOLISM | INHIBITION OF DNA AND RNA SYNTHESIS OR ACTIVITY
items.title: Mechanism of Action: Folate Pathway Inhibition by Sulfonamides and Trimethoprim
items.content: Sulfonamides inhibit dihydropteroate synthetase, blocking the addition of PABA to pteridine. This inhibition is due to their structural similarity to PABA, making them competitive enzyme substrates. Trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, preventing the conversion of dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate. These agents target sequential steps in bacterial folate synthesis, crucial for nucleic acid and protein production.
keyPoints: Sulfonamides inhibit dihydropteroate synthetase (DHPS).
Trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR).
Selective antibacterial effect due to mammalian cells' reliance on exogenous folate.
Activity may be reduced by high concentrations of end products like thymidine and purines.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · INHIBITION OF BACTERIAL METABOLISM | INHIBITION OF DNA AND RNA SYNTHESIS OR ACTIVITY

items.subtopic: INHIBITION OF DNA AND RNA SYNTHESIS OR ACTIVITY
items.title: Selective Bactericidal Activity: Quinolones and Rifamycins
items.content: Quinolones inhibit bacterial DNA synthesis by targeting DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, trapping enzyme-DNA complexes and causing lethal double-strand breaks. Rifamycins bind to the beta subunit of bacterial RNA polymerase, blocking mRNA elongation. Both classes exhibit high selectivity for bacterial enzymes over mammalian counterparts.
keyPoints: Quinolones inhibit DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, causing bactericidal activity through double-strand breaks.
Rifamycins bind to the beta subunit of bacterial RNA polymerase, inhibiting mRNA elongation.
Selective antibacterial action due to structural differences between bacterial and mammalian enzymes.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · INHIBITION OF BACTERIAL METABOLISM | INHIBITION OF DNA AND RNA SYNTHESIS OR ACTIVITY

items.subtopic: DISRUPTION OF MEMBRANE INTEGRITY | PHARMACOKINETICS AND PHARMACODYNAMICS | PHARMACOKINETICS (part 1)
items.title: Mechanism of Action: Polymyxins and Daptomycin
items.content: Polymyxins disrupt bacterial membranes by binding to lipopolysaccharides, while daptomycin creates channels in the cytoplasmic membrane leading to depolarization.
keyPoints: Polymyxins target both cytoplasmic and outer membranes of gram-negative bacteria.
Daptomycin binds cytoplasmic membranes of gram-positive bacteria in presence of calcium, causing potassium leakage and depolarization.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · DISRUPTION OF MEMBRANE INTEGRITY | PHARMACOKINETICS AND PHARMACODYNAMICS | PHARMACOKINETICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISRUPTION OF MEMBRANE INTEGRITY | PHARMACOKINETICS AND PHARMACODYNAMICS | PHARMACOKINETICS (part 1)
items.title: Bioavailability in Drug Administration
items.content: Bioavailability is crucial for non-IV routes, influenced by factors like food timing, metabolism, and first-pass effect.
keyPoints: Bioavailability defines the fraction of a drug reaching systemic circulation or action site.
IV administration has 100% bioavailability.
Oral bioavailability can be affected by food, enzymes, transporters, solubility, acid degradation, and conditions like diarrhea.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · DISRUPTION OF MEMBRANE INTEGRITY | PHARMACOKINETICS AND PHARMACODYNAMICS | PHARMACOKINETICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISRUPTION OF MEMBRANE INTEGRITY | PHARMACOKINETICS AND PHARMACODYNAMICS | PHARMACOKINETICS (part 1)
items.title: Drug Metabolism: Phase I and II Reactions
items.content: Drugs undergo phase I reactions to increase polarity for elimination, while phase II reactions further modify drugs into more polar compounds.
keyPoints: Phase I metabolism involves dealkylation, hydroxylation, oxidation, and deamination.
Phase II includes glucuronidation, sulfation, and acetylation.
CYP3A4 is a key enzyme in phase I reactions.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · DISRUPTION OF MEMBRANE INTEGRITY | PHARMACOKINETICS AND PHARMACODYNAMICS | PHARMACOKINETICS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Drug Excretion and Half-Life in Renal Impairment
items.content: Excretion is a key pharmacokinetic process involving drug elimination through renal clearance, biliary tract, or intestine. The half-life of a drug, which can range from minutes to days, is influenced by the rate of excretion. In patients with renal impairment, the half-life and overall drug clearance time are extended, necessitating dose adjustments to prevent toxicity.
keyPoints: Renal clearance includes glomerular filtration, tubular secretion, and passive diffusion.
Half-life is defined as the time it takes for blood concentration of a drug to decrease by one-half.
In renal impairment, dosing interval should be increased due to extended half-life.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · PHARMACOKINETICS (part 2) | PHARMACODYNAMICS

items.title: Pharmacodynamics: Optimizing Antibacterial Efficacy
items.content: Pharmacodynamics involves the relationship between drug concentrations and their efficacy or toxicity. For antibacterials, efficacy is related to parameters like concentration-dependent killing ($C_{max}/MIC$), time-dependent killing (T > MIC), and AUC/MIC. Different agents require specific pharmacodynamic targets for optimal effect; e.g., fluoroquinolones need an AUC/MIC ratio of >30 against Streptococcus pneumoniae.
keyPoints: Concentration-dependent killing is defined by the $C_{max}/MIC$ ratio.
Time-dependent killing requires drug concentrations to exceed MIC for a desired period (T > MIC).
AUC/MIC ratios: >30 for fluoroquinolones against Streptococcus pneumoniae, >125 for optimal effects against gram-negative pathogens.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · PHARMACOKINETICS (part 2) | PHARMACODYNAMICS

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THERAPY | EMPIRICAL AND DIRECTED THERAPY | SITE OF INFECTION
items.title: Empirical vs. Directed Therapy in Antibiotic Management
items.content: Empirical therapy is initiated when the causative pathogen of an infection is unknown, based on clinical severity and likely pathogens. It often involves broad-spectrum antibiotics to cover diverse potential agents until specific data are available. Directed therapy follows once the pathogen is identified, allowing for more targeted treatment with narrower spectrum antibiotics.
keyPoints: Empirical therapy is used when the causative agent is unknown.
Directed therapy relies on identification of the pathogen and its susceptibility profile.
Switching to directed therapy reduces risks of drug side effects and antibacterial resistance.
Dexamethasone may improve outcomes in acute bacterial meningitis but can reduce CSF penetration of some antibiotics like vancomycin.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · APPROACH TO THERAPY | EMPIRICAL AND DIRECTED THERAPY | SITE OF INFECTION

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THERAPY | EMPIRICAL AND DIRECTED THERAPY | SITE OF INFECTION
items.title: Considerations for Antibacterial Therapy Based on Infection Site
items.content: The site of infection significantly influences antibiotic choice due to varying drug penetration capabilities. For instance, meningitis treatment requires drugs that can cross the blood-brain barrier and achieve adequate CSF concentrations. Dexamethasone is used adjunctively in some cases but may affect vancomycin's efficacy, necessitating additional agents like rifampin.
keyPoints: Drug penetration varies by infection site; meningitis requires effective CSF concentration.
Dexamethasone can improve outcomes in bacterial meningitis but may reduce vancomycin penetration into the CSF.
Rifampin is added to counteract dexamethasone's effect on vancomycin penetration.
Other challenging sites include osteomyelitis, prostatitis, intraocular infections, and abscesses.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · APPROACH TO THERAPY | EMPIRICAL AND DIRECTED THERAPY | SITE OF INFECTION

items.title: Aggressive Treatment in Immune Dysfunction Patients
items.content: Patients with immune dysfunction, such as neutropenia or asplenia, are at increased risk for severe bacterial infections and should be treated aggressively. Initial treatment often involves broad-spectrum antibiotics to cover potential pathogens until specific microbiologic test results are available.
keyPoints: Immune dysfunction increases the risk of severe bacterial infection.
Asplenic patients require coverage against encapsulated organisms like S. pneumoniae.
Neutropenic patients typically receive antibacterial agents with broad activity against gram-negative bacteria.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · HOST FACTORS

items.title: Antibiotic Use in Pregnancy: Considerations and Risks
items.content: Pregnancy influences antibiotic therapy decisions due to increased risk of certain infections (e.g., Listeria) and potential fetal risks. Antibacterial agents are categorized by the FDA, with categories D and X contraindicated during pregnancy or lactation.
keyPoints: Pregnancy increases risk for specific infections like those caused by Listeria.
Antibacterial safety in pregnancy is grouped into FDA categories; drugs in categories D and X are contraindicated.
Post-2015, the Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Final Rule (PLLR) changed labeling practices.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · HOST FACTORS

items.subtopic: DURATION OF THERAPY | FAILURE OF THERAPY | EXPERT GUIDANCE | CLINICAL USE OF ANTIBACTERIAL AGENTS
items.title: Optimal Duration of Antibacterial Therapy
items.content: The duration of antibacterial therapy should be determined based on guidelines that consider the infecting organism, organ system involved, and patient-specific factors. Shorter courses are generally preferred to minimize adverse effects and reduce resistance.
keyPoints: Guidelines synthesize literature and expert opinion for therapy duration recommendations.
Shorter courses of therapy are favored where data exist to minimize drug side effects and bacterial resistance.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · DURATION OF THERAPY | FAILURE OF THERAPY | EXPERT GUIDANCE | CLINICAL USE OF ANTIBACTERIAL AGENTS

items.subtopic: DURATION OF THERAPY | FAILURE OF THERAPY | EXPERT GUIDANCE | CLINICAL USE OF ANTIBACTERIAL AGENTS
items.title: Approach to Failure of Antibacterial Therapy
items.content: If therapy fails, consider imaging and additional microbiologic testing. Causes may include inadequate regimen for the organism, resistance development, or poor penetration at infection sites. Surgical intervention might be necessary for certain infections.
keyPoints: Investigate with imaging and additional specimen collection if therapy fails.
Considerations: incorrect regimen, resistance development, poor systemic penetration.
Surgical interventions may be required for specific cases like large abscesses or myonecrosis.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · DURATION OF THERAPY | FAILURE OF THERAPY | EXPERT GUIDANCE | CLINICAL USE OF ANTIBACTERIAL AGENTS

items.title: Time-Dependent Killing of β-Lactams
items.content: The clinical efficacy of β-lactam antibiotics is best correlated with the proportion of the dosing interval during which drug levels remain above the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) for the targeted pathogen.
keyPoints: β-lactams are time-dependent killing agents.
Efficacy correlates with time drug levels exceed MIC.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · β-LACTAMS (part 1)

items.title: Use of β-Lactam-β-Lactamase Inhibitor Combinations
items.content: The rising prevalence of β-lactamase-producing bacteria has led to increased use of combinations like ampicillin-sulbactam and piperacillin-tazobactam for broader-spectrum coverage in infections such as pneumonia and intraabdominal infections.
keyPoints: β-lactam-β-lactamase inhibitors are used when broader spectrum is needed.
Examples include ampicillin-sulbactam, piperacillin-tazobactam.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · β-LACTAMS (part 1)

items.title: Cephalosporin Generations and Their Uses
items.content: First-generation cephalosporins are used for MSSA and streptococcal infections, while third-generation agents like ceftriaxone treat meningitis caused by H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, and susceptible S. pneumoniae strains.
keyPoints: First-gen: MSSA, streptococci (e.g., skin infections).
Third-gen: Ceftriaxone for meningitis (H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, susceptible S. pneumoniae).
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · β-LACTAMS (part 1)

items.title: Use of Ceftaroline for MRSA Infections
items.content: Ceftaroline is a fifth-generation cephalosporin with unique activity against methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), unlike other β-lactams.
keyPoints: Added activity against MRSA, which is resistant to all other β-lactams
Similar gram-negative activity to third-generation cephalosporins but excludes Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Clinical applications include community-acquired pneumonia and skin infections; emerging data support use in severe infections like bacteremia
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · β-LACTAMS (part 2)

items.title: Ceftolozane-Tazobactam for Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections
items.content: Ceftolozane-tazobactam is a novel cephalosporin-β-lactamase inhibitor combination effective against multidrug-resistant gram-negative bacteria.
keyPoints: Stable against many ESBL-producing organisms due to tazobactam component
Activity includes Pseudomonas and some anaerobes
Clinical benefit in complicated intraabdominal infections and urinary tract infections
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · β-LACTAMS (part 2)

items.title: Adverse Reactions to β-Lactam Drugs
items.content: Common adverse reactions to β-lactams include gastrointestinal side effects, hypersensitivity reactions, and rare cases of serum sickness or neurotoxicity.
keyPoints: Gastrointestinal side effects, mainly diarrhea, are common
Hypersensitivity reactions range from rash to anaphylaxis with a rate of true anaphylactic reactions at 0.05%
Neutropenia and interstitial nephritis generally resolve upon discontinuation
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · β-LACTAMS (part 2)

items.title: Durlobactam-Sulbactam for Multidrug-Resistant Acinetobacter Infections
items.content: Durlobactam, a novel diazabicyclooctane non-β-lactam β-lactamase inhibitor combined with sulbactam, is approved for treating multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter infections. It addresses hospital-acquired and ventilator-associated pneumonia caused by Acinetobacter baumannii-calcoaceticus complex.
keyPoints: Approved in the United States in 2023
Indicated for hospital-acquired and ventilator-associated pneumonia
Adverse reactions include abnormal liver function tests, diarrhea, anemia, and hypokalemia
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · SULBACTAM-DURLOBACTAM | GLYCOPEPTIDES AND LIPOGLYCOPEPTIDES

items.title: Vancomycin Use in MRSA Infections
items.content: Vancomycin is a glycopeptide antibiotic effective against MRSA and other gram-positive organisms. It is used for serious infections like pneumonia, bacteremia, osteomyelitis, and endocarditis. Monitoring vancomycin trough levels (15–20 µg/mL) is crucial to maintain efficacy (AUC/MIC ratio >400) while minimizing nephrotoxicity.
keyPoints: Effective against MRSA and other gram-positive organisms
Target trough level: 15-20 µg/mL for MRSA infections
Monitor for nephrotoxicity, especially with levels >20 µg/mL
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · SULBACTAM-DURLOBACTAM | GLYCOPEPTIDES AND LIPOGLYCOPEPTIDES

items.title: Lipoglycopeptides as Alternatives to Vancomycin
items.content: Telavancin, dalbavancin, and oritavancin are lipoglycopeptides with activity against MRSA, VISA, VRSA, streptococci, and enterococci. They offer similar efficacy to vancomycin for skin and soft tissue infections, with a low resistance rate. Telavancin requires caution due to potential nephrotoxicity and QTc prolongation.
keyPoints: Activity against MRSA, VISA, VRSA, streptococci, and enterococci
Low resistance rates among S. aureus and enterococcal isolates
Telavancin may cause nephrotoxicity and QTc prolongation
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · SULBACTAM-DURLOBACTAM | GLYCOPEPTIDES AND LIPOGLYCOPEPTIDES

items.title: Daptomycin Use and Monitoring for Toxicity
items.content: Daptomycin is a lipopeptide antibiotic effective against various gram-positive organisms, including MRSA. It's used in serious infections like bacteremia and endocarditis when vancomycin isn't suitable. Its main toxicity involves elevated CPK levels and myopathy; thus, monitoring CPK during treatment is crucial to prevent severe muscular toxicities.
keyPoints: Daptomycin is active against gram-positive organisms including MRSA and VRE.
Used for skin infections, bacteremia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis.
Main toxicity: elevated CPK levels and myopathy; monitor CPK during treatment.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · LIPOPEPTIDES | AMINOGLYCOSIDES

items.title: Aminoglycosides Dosing Strategy and Toxicity Concerns
items.content: Aminoglycosides, such as gentamicin and amikacin, exhibit concentration-dependent activity against gram-negative bacteria. Extended-interval dosing is employed to leverage their significant postantibiotic effect, reducing the frequency of administration while maintaining efficacy. However, they pose risks for nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity, with the latter potentially being irreversible.
keyPoints: Aminoglycosides are effective against gram-negative organisms like P. aeruginosa.
Extended-interval dosing is used due to their postantibiotic effect.
Toxicities include nephrotoxicity (usually reversible) and ototoxicity (potentially irreversible).
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · LIPOPEPTIDES | AMINOGLYCOSIDES

items.title: Azithromycin's Advantages Over Erythromycin in Respiratory Infections
items.content: Azithromycin is preferred over erythromycin for treating respiratory infections due to its better oral absorption, tolerability, and broader spectrum of activity. It covers atypical organisms like Chlamydophila pneumoniae and Legionella pneumophila effectively.
keyPoints: Better oral absorption and tolerability compared to erythromycin
Broader spectrum: effective against S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis, atypical organisms (Chlamydophila pneumoniae, Legionella pneumophila, Mycoplasma pneumoniae)
Used for prophylaxis and treatment of M. avium complex in HIV/AIDS patients
Azithromycin does not inhibit CYP3A4, avoiding drug interactions with benzodiazepines, statins, warfarin, cyclosporine, tacrolimus
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · MACROLIDES AND KETOLIDES | CLINDAMYCIN

items.title: Clindamycin's Role in Treating Anaerobic Infections and Toxin Synthesis Inhibition
items.content: Clindamycin is effective against anaerobic infections and is used to inhibit toxin synthesis in streptococcal or staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome. It has excellent oral bioavailability, making it suitable for various infections.
keyPoints: Bacteriostatic against some organisms, bactericidal against others
Effective against anaerobes (e.g., B. fragilis, Clostridium perfringens) and susceptible staphylococci and streptococci
Used for dental infections, anaerobic lung abscess, skin and soft tissue infections
Combines with bactericidal agents to inhibit toxin synthesis in toxic shock syndrome
Adverse effects: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, C. difficile-associated diarrhea, maculopapular rash, Stevens-Johnson syndrome
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · MACROLIDES AND KETOLIDES | CLINDAMYCIN

items.title: Indications for Tigecycline Use and Associated Risks
items.content: Tigecycline, a glycylcycline derivative of minocycline, is indicated for complicated skin and soft tissue infections, complicated intraabdominal infections, and community-acquired bacterial pneumonia in adults. It has broad activity against MRSA, vancomycin-sensitive enterococci, many Enterobacterales, and Bacteroides species but lacks efficacy against P. aeruginosa. However, a pooled analysis of 13 clinical trials indicated an increased risk of death and treatment failure when used alone, prompting the FDA to mandate a black box warning.
keyPoints: Tigecycline is approved for complicated skin and soft tissue infections, complicated intraabdominal infections, and community-acquired bacterial pneumonia in adults.
Activity against MRSA, vancomycin-sensitive enterococci, many Enterobacterales, and Bacteroides species; no activity against P. aeruginosa.
Increased risk of death and treatment failure when used alone (pooled analysis of 13 clinical trials).
FDA mandated a black box warning.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · TETRACYCLINES

items.title: Contraindications and Adverse Reactions of Tetracyclines
items.content: Tetracyclines, both old (doxycycline, minocycline, tetracycline) and new (tigecycline, eravacycline, omadacycline), are associated with major adverse reactions including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and photosensitivity. They should be avoided during pregnancy due to the risk of fetal bone-growth abnormalities and in children under 8 years old. Additionally, their oral absorption is reduced when coadministered with calcium- and iron-containing compounds; doses should be spaced at least 2 hours apart.
keyPoints: Major adverse reactions: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, photosensitivity.
Avoid during pregnancy due to risk of fetal bone-growth abnormalities.
Not recommended for children under 8 years old.
Reduced absorption with calcium- and iron-containing compounds; space doses at least 2 hours apart.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · TETRACYCLINES

items.title: Indications and Resistance Patterns of TMP-SMX
items.content: TMP-SMX is a broad-spectrum antibiotic effective against many gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, but its use is limited by resistance patterns. It's commonly used for urinary tract infections, skin and soft tissue infections, respiratory tract infections, and Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis in immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: TMP-SMX inhibits two steps in folate synthesis.
Active against gram-positive bacteria like staphylococci and streptococci; limited activity against MRSA.
Effective against many gram-negative bacteria including H. influenzae, E. coli, P. mirabilis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, S. maltophilia.
Not active against anaerobes or P. aeruginosa.
Resistance rates among urinary isolates of E. coli are almost 25% in the United States.
Common adverse reactions: gastrointestinal effects, rash, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, granulocytopenia, nephrotoxicity, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · TRIMETHOPRIM-SULFAMETHOXAZOLE | FLUOROQUINOLONES

items.title: Fluoroquinolones: Spectrum and Safety Considerations
items.content: Fluoroquinolones, including ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin, are broad-spectrum antibiotics effective against gram-negative bacteria and some gram-positive organisms. They exhibit concentration-dependent killing and have specific safety concerns such as CNS effects and tendinopathy.
keyPoints: Includes norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, ofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, gemifloxacin, delafloxacin.
Ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin have broad activity against gram-negative bacteria including P. aeruginosa.
Levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, gemifloxacin, and delafloxacin also cover some gram-positive organisms like S. pneumoniae and MSSA.
Strains of MRSA are commonly resistant to all fluoroquinolones except delafloxacin.
Used for bacterial prostatitis due to good prostate tissue penetration.
Adverse effects: CNS stimulatory effects, seizures, peripheral neuropathy, glucose dysregulation, tendinopathy, risk of aortic aneurysm, worsening myasthenia gravis.
Moxifloxacin causes modest QTc interval prolongation.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · TRIMETHOPRIM-SULFAMETHOXAZOLE | FLUOROQUINOLONES

items.title: Rifampin: Essential for Mycobacterial Infections and Prophylaxis
items.content: Rifampin is crucial in treating mycobacterial infections, particularly M. tuberculosis, where it's used as part of combination therapy or alone for latent infection. It also serves prophylactic roles against H. influenzae and N. meningitidis meningitis.
keyPoints: Used primarily for mycobacterial infections like M. tuberculosis
Combination therapy reduces high-level rifampin resistance risk
Prophylaxis in close contacts of patients with H. influenzae or N. meningitidis meningitis
Adverse effects: elevated aminotransferase levels (14%), rash (1–5%), gastrointestinal events (1–2%)
High oral bioavailability, enhanced on an empty stomach
Clinician must review patient's medications for drug interactions
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · RIFAMYCINS | METRONIDAZOLE

items.title: Metronidazole: Key Agent for Anaerobic and Protozoal Infections
items.content: Metronidazole is effective against anaerobic bacterial infections and certain protozoa, making it a choice for polymicrobial abscesses and conditions like bacterial vaginosis. It's also used for mild to moderate C. difficile-associated diarrhea.
keyPoints: Used for anaerobic bacterial infections and protozoal infections
Choice agent in combination therapy for polymicrobial abscesses
Effective against Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella species
Adverse effects: nausea, diarrhea, metallic taste; avoid alcohol due to disulfiram-like reaction
Long-term use risks leukopenia, neutropenia, peripheral neuropathy, CNS toxicity
CYP2C9 interaction with warfarin requires close monitoring
Increases serum lithium levels and can cause phenytoin toxicity
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · RIFAMYCINS | METRONIDAZOLE

items.title: Linezolid: Indications and Cautions
items.content: Linezolid is used for serious infections caused by resistant gram-positive bacteria like MRSA and VRE. It has excellent oral bioavailability but carries risks of myelosuppression, peripheral neuropathy (which may be irreversible), and serotonin syndrome when combined with SSRIs.
keyPoints: Indicated for MRSA and VRE
Adverse effects: myelosuppression, ocular/peripheral neuropathy
Peripheral neuropathy may be irreversible
Weak monoamine oxidase inhibitor; avoid sympathomimetics and tyramine-rich foods
Risk of serotonin syndrome with SSRIs
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · OXAZOLIDINONES | PLEUROMUTILINS | NITROFURANTOIN

items.title: Nitrofurantoin: Use in UTIs and Precautions
items.content: Nitrofurantoin is bactericidal, effective against a range of gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, primarily used for urinary tract infections. It's preferred during pregnancy but has growing resistance issues in some regions. Avoid coadministration with magnesium and monitor elderly patients due to increased adverse reaction risk.
keyPoints: Bactericidal; concentration-dependent activity
Effective against S. aureus, E. coli, etc.
Preferred for UTIs in pregnancy
Avoid with magnesium; take with food

Not recommended for elderly due to increased adverse reactions

items.title: Polymyxins: Use in Multi-Drug Resistant Infections and Associated Toxicities
items.content: Polymyxins, including colistin and polymyxin B, disrupt bacterial cell membrane integrity. They are effective against nonenteric pathogens like P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii but not Burkholderia or gram-positive bacteria. These drugs are bactericidal, available in IV formulations, and used for infections resistant to other antibiotics such as urinary tract infections, hospital-acquired pneumonia, and bloodstream infections. Nebulized forms can treat refractory ventilator-associated pneumonia and prevent infections in bronchiectasis/cystic fibrosis patients.
keyPoints: Active against P. aeruginosa, A. baumannii; ineffective against Burkholderia and gram-positive bacteria.
Used for multi-drug resistant infections: UTIs, hospital-acquired pneumonia, bloodstream infections.
Most important adverse effect is dose-dependent reversible nephrotoxicity.
Less common neurotoxicity includes paresthesias, muscle weakness, confusion.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · POLYMYXINS | QUINUPRISTIN-DALFORPRISTIN | FOSFOMYCIN

items.title: Quinupristin-Dalfopristin: Spectrum and Adverse Effects
items.content: Quinupristin-dalfopristin, a combination of streptogramins, inhibits protein synthesis. It is effective against staphylococci (including MRSA), streptococci, E. faecium, Corynebacterium species, and L. monocytogenes but not gram-negative organisms reliably. The drug is used for vancomycin-resistant E. faecium infections and other gram-positive bacterial infections, including UTIs, bone/joint infections, and bacteremia.
keyPoints: Kills bacteria by inhibiting protein synthesis; active against MRSA, streptococci, E. faecium (not E. faecalis).
Used for vancomycin-resistant E. faecium and other gram-positive infections.
Adverse effects include infusion-related reactions, arthralgias, myalgias (may require discontinuation).
Inhibits CYP3A4 enzyme, leading to potential drug interactions.

items.title: When to Use Chloramphenicol and Its Risks
items.content: Chloramphenicol is reserved for specific infections when other treatments are contraindicated or ineffective, due to its serious toxicities. It's used for meningitis by susceptible bacteria like N. meningitidis, H. influenzae, and S. pneumoniae, among others. However, it carries risks such as aplastic anemia, myelosuppression, and gray baby syndrome.
keyPoints: Chloramphenicol is used for infections including meningitis by susceptible bacteria (N. meningitidis, H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae).
Adverse reactions include aplastic anemia, myelosuppression, and gray baby syndrome.
Inhibits CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 enzymes, increasing levels of many drugs.

items.title: Principles for Effective Antibacterial Prophylaxis
items.content: Antibacterial prophylaxis should be used selectively, based on well-designed studies or expert recommendations. It is crucial that the risk of infection outweighs the adverse effects of therapy and potential resistance. Timing and duration are critical: drugs should be administered within 1 hour before surgery for maximal effect, with redosing during prolonged procedures but not after wound closure.
keyPoints: Prophylaxis indicated only in selected circumstances supported by studies or expert recommendations.
Risk of infection must outweigh adverse effects and resistance potential.
Antibacterial drug delivery should be within 1 hour before surgery for maximal effect.
Redosing may be necessary during prolonged procedures but not after wound closure.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · CHLORAMPHENICOL | APPROACH TO PROPHYLAXIS OF INFECTION

items.title: Why Antimicrobial Stewardship is Crucial in Healthcare Settings
items.content: Antimicrobial stewardship programs are essential for promoting appropriate antimicrobial use, preserving healthcare resources by curbing resistance, reducing adverse effects, and controlling costs. These multidisciplinary teams implement strategies like prospective audit with intervention, formulary restriction, and preauthorization to optimize antimicrobial use.
keyPoints: Antimicrobial stewardship promotes appropriate drug selection, dosage, route, and duration of therapy.
Strategies include prospective audit with feedback, formulary restriction, and preauthorization.
Successful interventions have led to declines in broad-spectrum drug use and decreases in C. difficile infections.
items.reference: Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bacterial Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL STEWARDSHIP

items.title: How Antimicrobial Stewardship Strategies Improve Patient Outcomes
items.content: Antimicrobial stewardship strategies such as prospective audit, formulary restriction, and preauthorization lead to improved patient care by optimizing antimicrobial use. These interventions result in reduced adverse events like C. difficile infections and better drug susceptibility patterns.
keyPoints: Prospective audits review broad-spectrum antimicrobials for appropriateness and suggest alternatives when necessary.
Formulary restriction limits the use of antimicrobials to those with demonstrated benefit, reducing unnecessary expenditure.
Preauthorization requires approval before using selected antimicrobials, often facilitated by CPOE software or infectious disease specialists.
